# **CHAPTER III** **DESIGN AND METHODOLOGY**

This chapter of the paper presents an overview of the proposed study's fundamental theoretical foundations. The research framework is guided by these foundations in order to give substantial results to the research questions at the conclusion of the study. It was also utilized to monitor the progression of the system's workflow.

## **Research Design**

| When writing the discussion on research design and methodology in your thesis, it is essential to provide a clear and concise explanation of the chosen research methods and their relevance to your study. Begin by introducing and defining each methodology, such as developmental research, applied research, and descriptive research (descriptive evaluative), emphasizing their distinct characteristics. Then, seamlessly transition into a discussion of how these methods are applied within the context of your research. Explain why you selected these specific methodologies, highlighting their alignment with your research objectives and their practical implications. Emphasize the role each method plays in addressing key research questions or challenges. Ultimately, demonstrate how the combination of these methodologies creates a comprehensive and well-rounded approach to investigate your chosen topic, offering both theoretical insights and practical solutions. vvv |
| ----- |

Developmental research is applied to track the progression of the application's development and align the results with the research objective in order to address the budgeting behavior of Filipino young professionals.

We intend to apply this research into real-world problems by utilizing the practicality of a web-based, personal budget management system in both mobile and desktop for easier accessibility (Arinze, 2024; Chahar et al., 2026). Filipino young professionals face certain budget management variables in the context of incomes and cultural obligations, thus will be taken into account for budgeting (Rosario, 2025).

The Decision Tree algorithm will be used for the financial behavioral profile classification module and will act as a foundation. Odin takes cultural and obligation-driven budgets into account, which is not common in Western finance frameworks. Afterwards, it will utilize LSTM for the expense forecasting module due to it being the most pronounced and non-linear amongst other alternatives. The system will then use linear programming for the budget recommendation module as it can be designed to assist users for irregular pay cycles and variable incomes. Lastly, Isolation Forest will be utilized for the overspending detection module to identify and isolate anomalies through random partitioning. Related literatures have validated and preferred this unsupervised method for personal finance contexts with cold-start constraints.

Descriptive research will also allow for a comprehensive examination of the system's effectiveness. Users' data will be combined and gathered for detailed testing to come up with accurate results whilst utilizing machine learning algorithms, thus the application offers a comprehensive and practical solution to the target demographic.

## **Research Methodology** 

### **Agile/ GDLC (Pre-production , Production, Post Production)**

| When crafting the research methodology section in your thesis, specifically highlighting the use of the (example used is Agile) development method, it's essential to present a concise and structured explanation. Begin by introducing the section's significance, emphasizing that Agile aligns with the research objectives. Clearly define Agile, emphasizing its principles, and practices. Explain how Agile's iterative and incremental nature is applied within your research context, detailing how tasks are divided into sprints, fostering collaboration, open communication, and flexibility. Highlight the benefits of Agile in research, such as adaptability and stakeholder involvement, while acknowledging potential limitations. Conclude by reaffirming how Agile's suitability and adaptability make it an ideal methodology for achieving the dynamic research objectives. vvv |
| ----- |

We will utilize Agile methodology in developing the application as it provides an adaptive and dynamic framework suited for the mutually dependent nature of Odin’s development tasks. This will be built up of six distinct phases.

### **Planning Phase**

The project's objectives are defined with an outlined scope of Odin, ensuring that the system is tailored in the context of the Filipino young professionals, as well as defining non-negotiable expense categories like culturally obligated spending (Gavino et al., 2023; Donato et al., 2023; Casiño et al., 2025). We will also be establishing a requirement of mobile-first design for better accessibility whilst respecting user privacy and safety and utilizing colors that are commonly associated with their respective symbolisms (Khandelwal and Chaudhary, 2023; Arinze, 2024).

### **Design Phase**

| It's crucial that we incorporate model or algorithm development and evaluation seamlessly into the Design, Develop, and Test phases of our research methodology. In the Design phase, elucidate not only the application's architecture but also the fundamental algorithms and models, ensuring their alignment with research objectives and user requisites. When narrating the Develop phase, emphasize the simultaneous evolution of both the application's features and the core algorithms, underscoring the iterative and adaptable nature of our approach. During the Test phase, elucidate the procedures for rigorous algorithm evaluation, validation, and usability testing, involving Arnis practitioners and instructors for constructive feedback. This comprehensive approach ensures our research remains dynamic, user-centric, and dedicated to delivering a high-quality Arnis pose scoring application. vvv |
| ----- |

Odin is designed to have a modern, easy to use, and adaptable layout, cleanly displaying all the important details especially in the dashboard view. It has a leek-green and white as the main color palette and uses common color-codes to symbolize and highlight details such as green for Income, red for Expenses, and yellow for Savings, eliminating the need for users to memorize certain colors as it follows the standards for color psychology. The choices of colors also contribute to how a user reacts to certain information emotionally, such as but not limited to when the remaining balance is low (Khandelwal and Chaudhary, 2023).

#### **System Structure**

| Application Design: Describe the design of the Arnis pose scoring application. Outline the user interface (UI) and user experience (UX) design elements, considering factors such as ease of use, accessibility, and compatibility with different devices. Include wireframes, mockups, or sketches if available to visually represent the application's layout and functionality. Discuss how the application's design supports the seamless integration of the algorithm or model and how it caters to the needs and preferences of Arnis practitioners and instructors. vvv |
| ----- |

#### **Application Design**

Odin employs a multi-service architecture consisting of a frontend application, a primary backend, and an ML microservice. The frontend is built using React Native, React, React Native for Web, and Expo SDK, targeting Android mobile and web browser environments from a single codebase. TypeScript is used across the frontend and primary backend. Node.js with Express.js handles the primary backend, while a FastAPI microservice written in Python serves as the ML inference layer. PostgreSQL via Supabase provides the database and authentication infrastructure. NativeWind, Tailwind CSS, @expo/vector-icons, and React Native Paper handle interface styling and components. Git and GitHub are used for version control throughout.

ponents. Git and GitHub are used for version control throughout.

| ![][image9] | ![][image10] | ![][image11] |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| ![][image12] | ![][image13] | ![][image14] |

### **Development Phase**

We will be using the Agile incremental approach to develop the application, initially focusing on the more important features such as the Financial Behavioral Profile Assignment, Transaction History, Spending Forecasts, Overage Detection, and Budget Recommendation, then gradually developing the system and improving its overall usability. Agile development is cited to work successfully for mobile-first applications due to having clear objective establishment and resource allocation. Mobile-first application ensures the features work properly before being implemented on different resolutions to ensure proper responsiveness and smoother workflow (Arinze, 2024; Parameswaran and Saad, 2025).

| Algorithm Development/Model Training: Provide a comprehensive account of the algorithm development or model training process. Explain in detail how the algorithm is being implemented or how the model is being trained using the selected techniques and data. Include relevant code snippets, algorithms, or methodologies that are central to the development of the model. Clarify the steps taken to fine-tune the algorithm or model to achieve accuracy and reliability in assessing objectives. Highlight any challenges encountered and the strategies employed to overcome them. vvv |
| ----- |

#### **Algorithm/Model Training**

LSTM (Spending Forecasting). The LSTM model is trained on per-category historical transaction sequences to predict spending across daily, weekly, and monthly time horizons, using walk-forward validation to preserve temporal order. For users without sufficient transaction history, the model defaults to a profile-average baseline cold-start forecast, surfaced to the user with a visible indicator. Performance is evaluated using Mean Absolute Error (MAE), Symmetric Mean Absolute Percentage Error (SMAPE), Mean Directional Accuracy (MDA), and Root Mean Square Error (RMSE).

Classification Algorithm (Profile Classification). The classification model is trained to assign users to one of four profiles — Stable-Flexible, Stable-Obligated, Variable-Flexible, and Variable-Obligated — based on onboarding features, with progressive reclassification triggered by sustained behavioral shifts in accumulated transaction data. Performance is evaluated using Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and F1-Score.

Recommendation Algorithm (Budget Recommendation). The recommendation model is trained to generate personalized budget allocations constrained by the user's income, profile, and preferences, with minimum floors enforced for protected expense categories. Performance is evaluated using Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE), Root Mean Square Error (RMSE), and Variance Analysis.

Isolation Forest (Overage Detection). The Isolation Forest model is trained on individual user transaction feature vectors to establish per-user behavioral baselines without labeled anomaly data, operating alongside the rule-based overage alert layer. Performance is evaluated using Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and F1-Score.

The output of this phase is a set of trained and evaluated ML models with documented performance metrics across all four evaluation dimensions, satisfying Specific Objective 4\.

| Application or Software Development: Describe the development of the application or software. Discuss the programming languages, frameworks, and tools used for coding the application's features. If applicable, provide code snippets, architectural diagrams, or system flowcharts to elucidate the application's development process. Explain how the application interfaces with the algorithm or model, ensuring that the model is seamlessly integrated into the user-facing application. Detail the iterations, testing, and debugging phases in the development cycle, emphasizing the adherence to Agile principles of iterative improvement and user-centered design. vvv |
| ----- |

#### **Application Development**

The following eight features are implemented during this phase:

Financial Behavioral Profile Classification. Users are classified into one of four profiles — Stable-Flexible, Stable-Obligated, Variable-Flexible, or Variable-Obligated — during onboarding based on income type, income stability, and obligation weight, with automatic reclassification triggered by sustained behavioral shifts.  
Dashboard. A consolidated view of the user's financial status, displaying a spending forecast summary, recent transactions, per-category budget health, and active overage alerts.

Transaction Entry. Manual income and spending transaction entry using a FIES/BSP-grounded category structure organized into four groups — Essential, Discretionary, Obligatory, and Financial — with support for scheduled recurring transactions.

Spending Forecasting. LSTM-driven predicted spending totals and per-category breakdowns for daily, weekly, and monthly horizons, with a cold-start fallback forecast for users without sufficient transaction history.

Budget Recommendation. Personalized budget allocation plans anchored to the user's profile, income, preferences, and transaction history, with minimum floors for protected expense categories.

Overage Detection. Rule-based categorical overage alerts combined with Isolation Forest behavioral deviation detection, distinguishing culturally expected high-value expenditures from genuine unplanned anomalies.

Savings Goal Tracking. User-defined savings targets with cumulative progress tracking and long-term goal completion projections based on current saving rates.

Debt Management. Presentation and tracking of both the Avalanche and Snowball debt repayment strategies, allowing users to select and monitor their preferred approach.

The output of this phase is a functional Odin mobile and web application with all eight system features implemented and integrated, satisfying Specific Objective 5\.

### **Testing Phase**

Data gathering is a core task for this phase as they are needed to train our algorithms to provide more accurate results. In order to test Odin, the system is designed to rely on manual transactions and inputting as the data from the user needs to be as accurate and secure as possible. They are then compiled to match real-world manual inputs categorized to localized frameworks. Finally, they will be preprocessed to handle missing values and normalize features to structure data into suitable model training (Ghojogh and Ghodsi, 2023; Reddy et al., 2026).

| Algorithm Evaluation/Model Evaluation: Provide a detailed account of the evaluation process for the developed algorithm or trained model. Describe the metrics, datasets, and validation techniques used to assess the accuracy, precision, recall, and other relevant performance indicators. Explain how the algorithm or model performs when tested against diverse test instances, highlighting its strengths and potential limitations. Discuss the adjustments made based on evaluation results to enhance the algorithm's effectiveness in predicting or classifying accurately. vvv |
| ----- |

#### **Algorithm/Model Evaluation**

Module-level algorithm performance metrics from Phase 4, MAE, SMAPE, MDA, and RMSE for the LSTM module; Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and F1-Score for the classification and Isolation Forest modules; and MAPE, RMSE, and Variance Analysis for the recommendation module, are reported as part of the evaluation record alongside the system-level findings.

| System Testing: Elaborate on the system testing procedures conducted on the application. Explain how the integrated system, which includes both the algorithm or model and the user-facing software, is rigorously tested. Detail the testing scenarios, test cases, and test data used to evaluate the application's overall functionality, usability, and reliability. Address the testing of different user scenarios and potential edge cases to ensure that the application operates seamlessly under various conditions. Describe how user feedback and usability testing results have influenced system refinements and improvements. vvv |
| ----- |

#### **Functional Testing**

Functional tests verify correct behavior of each system module against defined test cases. The modules covered are the financial behavioral profile module, dashboard module, transaction entry module, spending forecasting module, budget recommendation module, overage detection module, savings goal tracking module, and debt management module.

#### **Non-Functional Testing**

Non-functional tests verify that the following performance standards are met: response time standards must be less than or equal to 2–3 seconds, latency thresholds must be less than or equal to 500–800ms under normal load; 800–1,000ms under elevated load, throughput standards must be met, concurrent user stability must be enforced under expected peak usage of 15-100 concurrent users, load testing and stress testing must be successfully met, error rate standards must be 0-2%, and test duration for sustained load scenarios of 5–15 minutes must be met.

The output of this phase is a complete set of functional test results and non-functional performance benchmarks, satisfying Specific Objective 6\.

System quality is assessed using two complementary instruments. ISO/IEC 25010:2023 evaluates software quality across several dimensions. For the visual layout: user interface aesthetics, accessibility, and appropriateness recognizability are assessed. For the interface element design: operability, user error protection, accessibility, and appropriateness recognizability are assessed. For system behavior: functional suitability, operability, performance efficiency, and user error protection are assessed.

The System Usability Scale (SUS) is administered alongside ISO/IEC 25010:2023 to capture a holistic measure of user-perceived usability through its standardized ten-item questionnaire, producing a composite score benchmarkable against established usability standards.

The output of this phase is a complete ISO/IEC 25010:2023 and SUS evaluation report from Filipino young professional respondents, satisfying Specific Objective 7\.

### **Deployment Phase**

Odin will then be deployed to be used by Filipino young professionals for personal use. With the use of Agile development, the system will continue to be improved whilst taking into account the new data given by the users from time to time to train the models, further enhancing usability and functionality.

### **Review Phase**

Odin needs at least 3 months of data to gain higher accuracy in prediction for LSTM to analyze (Reddy et al., 2026). To further enhance the models' capabilities, it will constantly loop back to Phase 3 until it achieves consistent results based on the users' entries.
